Boxhead Plastics exists to support the auto industry taking greater responsibility for its plastic waste; making 2000 golf tees out of one car bumper bar. We increase the sustainability of the auto sector reducing pressure on our governments to have to tackle this plastic problem
Although dependent on the environment, golf courses are often criticized for their negative impact on their surroundings. From high water usage to pollution caused by fertilizers and the loss of natural habitats. The Naiteev golf tee offers the golf industry a way to increase its environmental management of the game through the sale of our golf products
other_notes:

We are stimulating our local recycling and plastics economy by choosing a closed loop supply chain of local waste collectors (JK Plastics), processors (Astron), and manufacturers (Plastic Precise Products) to produce recycled plastic feedstock for injection mould manufacturing. What is best practice? 
Best practice helps people to get on with life easily, and with dignity. It gives them information they can use to do the ordinary things of life they need and want to do. 
Best practice helps people to: 
easily find what they are looking for, whether it’s available on paper or online 
easily exercise their rights 
receive information they can act on in their own and their 
community’s interest 
pay bills straightforwardly 
query any matter straightforwardly 
safely use dangerous products 
find out how to get from one place to another 
request and get benefits they are entitled to, easily and simply get compensation they deserve without trauma or guilt 
buy products they want easily… 
…and do all of these things to an agreed high standard, 
demonstrated with credible before and after evidence.

Vu Consulting is a PR agency driven by impact. We are a team of 5, all with extensive experience working on PR programs for corporate, B2B, tech, and startup businesses. After years at multiple agencies and in-house roles, and most recently leading the Australian arm of a global tech PR agency, Mylan Vu started Vu Consulting earlier this year to deliver PR services to tech/corporate/startup businesses in a more effective way than traditional agencies. This includes exclusively hiring senior staff, saying no to typical agency models of hierarchy and processes like weekly internal and client meetings, and being stringent about the types of clients and client work we work on. 

Most importantly, the biggest difference is this only takes up 50% of our time. Having spent years with the media industry, Mylan and her team have seen first-hand what the research shows – that the most accessible and regularly reported and referenced ‘experts’ in Australian media are middle-aged, white, and male. This has a domino effect on who has power in our society, who influences what others think and know, and how messages are communicated. It influences policies, leadership, and social and corporate culture.  

This is why Vu Consulting spends the other 50% of our time doing what we call ‘giving a voice to the voiceless’. While our tech clients help us pay the bills, we also work with NFPs, charities, and social enterprises on a pro bono basis to reflect the diversity of our communities while also elevating the people, organisations, and issues that are too commonly overlooked or misrepresented in our media. We believe the media can and must do a better job of reflecting all the issues in our society, not just the ones seemingly important to those already in power. And we believe our PR services can help to generate this change. 

Our pro bono work particularly focuses on working with organisations that are too small to have their own in-house marketing or PR teams, or too low on resources to afford to pay for external consultants. They also are typically passionate about advocating for social change, and we work with several clients purely on advocacy and thought leadership activities. Our pro bono clients span across various issues including domestic violence, refugee rights, disability, racism and discrimination, pregnancy loss, digital inclusion, and social justice reform. 

Our typical services include media relations, content development, thought leadership, corporate profiling, social media content, media training, and website copy.

project_scope:
We are looking for the Student Consulting Team to complete a Growth Strategy / New Market Entry Strategy Project for our organisation, in particular: [please choose max 4 points from the list below]
Research the Political, Economic, Social / Cultural, Technological and Environmental factors & trends of specific relevance to the product / service
Develop persona and value canvas for the potential customers
Segmentation, size, growth and value of target market
Review competing or substitute products / services
Outline potential barriers to entry 
Identify potential suppliers and partners
Analyse the industry life cycle in a selected country
Identify market entry cost estimates
SWOT and 5 Forces analysis
In particular in the above, to deliver the most value, we would like the team to focus most effort on;
We would like Practera’s help in assessing 2 specific areas – domestic violence and refugee rights. The research should ultimately help us understand how we can raise public awareness of the complexities of these issues and ideal actions taken to address each issue, primarily through media and social media channels. We are looking for research that will help us determine can PR can drive real, long-term social change.
And avoid
We are not looking for ways to make a ‘splash’ around a brand or an issue, as we have the experience and knowledge to do this already. For example, we aren’t interested in campaigns that involve making a hashtag viral, or creating startling video content with celebrity influencers.
other_notes:
Please feel free to provide more detail and context under these points to make them suitable for your company.

Research the Political, Economic, Social / Cultural, Technological and Environmental factors & trends of specific relevance to the product / service
Looking separately at the issues of domestic violence and refugee rights, we’d like to understand: 
How big is each sector in terms of the government funding received, organisations and NFPs in each space, how ‘popular’ they are for donations, etc., and how does this compare to other social issues?
What are the current initiatives, laws, bills that are on the table in government regarding these issues? Who are the main players in pushing these forward? How have these evolved over time? What other initiatives/laws/bills are the sector trying to have tabled – where are they up to?
How are different stages in legislation development/publication covered in the media? At what point are media interested in covering it, who is driving the conversation and how? What were the stages of it going through industry bodies, FairWork or otherwise? What kinds of companies spoke about it or commented on it? What is the role of media/petitioning throughout the process?

Develop persona and value canvas for the potential customers

Which media/journalists have been covering these issues over a long period of time – i.e. multiple months/years. How have these journalists adopted their approaches and angles over time?
What are the common themes/approaches in media articles covering these issues – e.g. we find ABC articles tend to require a case study, even if it’s anonymous, Mamamia articles tend to prefer first-person accounts of personal experiences
Which journalists have started writing on these issues more recently? 
Which publications/journalists tend to have a particular view on these issues and what is that view? i.e. are there certain publications that are ‘pro’ or ‘anti’ refugee rights? 

Segmentation, size, growth and value of target market

Discussed above. 

Review competing or substitute products / services

Are their particular arguments, people, organisations, or otherwise that could cause roadblocks for advocating on domestic violence and refugee rights issues? E.g. In our work on youth support issues, we have found it challenging to comment while youth crime is reportedly severe, particularly in QLD and journalists seem to prefer to cover the crime stories rather than the advocacy voices for youth support.
deliverables:
An oral presentation of the completed project.
A report summarizing your findings and recommendations.

client_background:
Vision: : Mission To empower consumers to solve problems and create a better world through their purchasing behaviour.
We do this by creating a curated list of Social Enterprises (SE) and their products on a central platform for consumers to make informed purchase decisions according to their current purchase need or according to the impact they wish to create. This gives consumers a chance to make a social impact through their everyday purchases and use their spend for good. 
The Vision is to create a trusted and transparent (impact) marketplace of choice for socially conscious consumers with a wide range of products and services where social enterprises can reach a validated and engaged audience to sell their products and services and highlight their social, economic and environmental messages and the impact they are creating. 

Customers: Males and females with disposable income who want to make purchase decisions that provides them with a high quality product at a price comparable to the rest of the market for that standard of product where the Social Enterprise makes a measurable impact on a cause.
The market gap is a central repository of SEs that are selling goods and are making social and environmental impact already. I am helping my time poor target market to easily find the cause they want to support, learn more about the cause and buy something supporting that cause.
OR I want my time poor customers to find the product they were going to buy anyway – but offer them a choice of the same product from a company featured on G4G.
People who are in the workforce who are time poor because they have started or are establishing their career and don’t have the time to help with the environment or social causes between work, their lifestyle choices, their socialising. 
Target market will also be identified according to product categories ie. Specific products will be marketed to a different audience depending on their likelihood to buy the product. Eg. Plastic bracelets to clean up the ocean is more for 18 to 20s year olds. For groceries and gifts, the main decision maker in the household is the mum. 

Products: I have a number of products in many different categories available from homeware, jewelry, food and drinks. All of them are Social Enterprises that are profit with purpose business. Each one has a measurable impact area.

English is widely used as either first or second language
Autism is acknowledged

client_background: The Cares Family is a network of charities which work to tackle loneliness and to promote social connection in cities across the UK. We provide a number of programmes which bring older and younger neighbours together to share time, laughter, and new experiences and offer practical support to social entrepreneurs seeking to nurture relationships and bridge social divides within communities. We also advocate for policy action to build social capital, and played a significant role in campaigning for and shaping the government's first ever strategy to combat loneliness.
problem_statement: The client seeks to improve their current market position by exploring innovative solutions to emerging challenges in the industry.
project_scope:
We are looking for the Student Consulting Team to do comparative policy-related research for our organisation, including:
Summarise key information on our organisation, target policy area, and policy in question
Analyse current global or local policy landscape relevant to the brief
Identify the top 5 comparable policies of interest and, using secondary research, provide relevant insights into each policy’s attributes (e.g. policy creation and implementation, policy communication, stakeholder response and compliance, and policy outcomes)
Based on the research and evaluation of comparable policies, identify potential opportunities and limitations for your client to consider for the policy relevant to this brief
focus_area:
In particular in the above, to deliver the most value, we would like the team to focus most effort on;
We would like the student group to 1) undertake the desk research project described above (focused on policy approaches to generating social capital, and bridging social capital in particular) and 2) support the design of our planned workshops with The Cares Family staff and programme participants. If possible and workshop dates align, we would like to invite you to join the workshop.

And avoid
We're specifically interested in how - or the techniques and tactics through which - communities and civic institutions generate social capital. This policy project shouldn't be focused on why social capital matters. It might be useful to a degree to explore competing conceptualisations of social capital, but only inasmuch as this will enhance our understanding of those techniques and tactics!
Should the team focus on your local policy landscape (i.e., regional, national) or on international policies (and if so, please identify particular countries of interest?)
I think we'd be interested in taking an international view, especially given that the literature on policy approaches to boosting social capital within the UK is (I think) relatively limited.
Please identify whether the team should focus their research on certain policy attributes or phase of policy development (e.g. creation and implementation, communication, compliance) 
I suppose this is more of a creation and implementation thing, but we'd be grateful for any support and would be more than happy to work with Practera, LSE and the students to make this a mutually beneficial collaboration!
Please feel free to provide more detail and context under these points to make them suitable for your company.
I think the best jumping-off point in visualising this policy product is pages 48-49 of the report on levelling up which we produced last year (https://files.thecaresfamily.org.uk/thecaresfamily/images/Building-our-social-infrastructure-Final.pdf). This reflects the core approach which we'd want to follow in exploring the mechanisms for building social connection which The Cares Family employs in its programme delivery work, though it's almost completely devoid of the theoretical and comparative policy analysis which I'd view as a crucial component of a credible 'what works' policy product.

Ultimately, we want to push the government to draw a distinction in its social capital policy work between community institutions in which local people might incidentally come into contact with one another and those in which they are purposefully brought together to meet and mix in consequential ways, including across perceived lines of difference (‘connecting institutions’). We believe that sharing space enables people to develop weak ties and – as a consequence – important feelings of familiarity, comfort and security; but that sharing positive and meaningful experiences allows them to form associational relationships and – if structured appropriately – to discover commonalities with others from different backgrounds and generations. This is the larger advocacy narrative which we are hoping to advance through this policy project!
deliverables: Provide a Final Report and Presentation

client_background:
Mission: A Lasting Tale enables you to record the audio life story of your loved one. 
Vision: Our vision / There are 125 million people in the world over the age of 80. Let’s hear their collective voice, one audio life story at a time.
Customers: Customers: Retail targeted towards family members of elderly and dying; elderly and dying themselves; aged care residential homes; home care providers; palliative care homes and palliative care organisations
Products: Audio and video life stories. Mobile app (search A Lasting Tale to download) records a free 30 minute guided life story which can be stored for $5 a year or customers can choose not to store. To be piloted as a subscription personalised service to aged care sector. Then after free or stored steps up to Professional Audio Stories - $1299 - see App and website for full details of product offering. And then Professional Video Stories - $3599 - see App and website for full details of product offering. And then Professional Video Stories. 
Competitors: •	
•	USA’s StoryCorps - public charity collecting wide-scale oral history. Great, but US-specific and not focused on elderly market, plus there is no option to turn it into a professional audio story.  
•	UK’s Hospice Biographers recording audio life stories, but no central app like us for management. This could be a potential partner for us.
•	Memento App - launched recently in Australia. Not as strong a focus on aged care more on specialised oral history.
•	Range of audio recording apps, written books from apps/websites doing scale life story collection (in US, UK and Aus) therefore a demonstrable market.
•	Professionals and volunteers conduct audio and video life history interviews/life stories in homes, palliative care etc here and globally, most of these are individuals or individual organisations, and they are not managed centrally. Again, ALT’s centralised system means potential partnering
•	“Digital vaults” e.g My Life Capsule exist.

Business Model: Optional storage of free audio life story interviews - $5 a year
Professional Audio - $1299 - doing this for two years - 60 interviews
Professional Video - $3599 - not yet launched
Workshops - present 2-hour workshops training people how to do this.
Challenges: Ability to deliver on scaling options available via newly launched App. Have the ability to roll out interviews across Australia and the Globe via a freelance interview model but not the capacity. We have received a small amount of funding from the NSW Government to do three free pilots in aged care homes, home care providers and health networks so we need the capabilities and capacity to run these, report and then leverage opportunities from these free pilots.
problem_statement: The client seeks to improve their current market position by exploring innovative solutions to emerging challenges in the industry.

focus_area:

In particular in the above, to deliver the most value, we would like the team to focus most effort on;
Scalability and market: The challenge listed above plus research and insights on how life story collection can be scaled using A Lasting Tale in the aged care sector. How do we take my app and make it used by every single home care provider and aged care residential provider in Australia? 
And avoid
There are big pivots and other markets audio can be collected outside of elderly and dying, but I would like the focus of the research to be on the current market I have identified. I am always open to ideas about new markets, but not a focus on that. Also, large scale changes to the App at the MVP stage cannot really happen from a funding and tech. perspective.
Further details: 
Here is our value proposition: A Lasting Tale enables each of us to record the audio life story of our loved elderly and dying, in the home or in care. With your family, a friend, or a carer. Our centralised technology allows the stories of the 125 million people in the world over 80 to record an audio life story across all price points and technology. We provide the questions and the know how to extract the best and lasting memories. Our technology still enables face to face interviews, so that our elderly can share their stories and really connect. Our guided questions, the various access points, and the voice on elderly voices is what makes us unique.
other_notes: App can be download from Apple Play and Google Store search for A Lasting Tale or access download links from website alastingtale.com

Vision: TEDxUWA hopes to facilitate a more interconnected and harmonious conversation between multiple disciplines within the local UWA community and the wider Perth community.
Mission: TEDxUWA aims to spread good ideas among the UWA community; creating a synergy of ideas between different faculties and people, through our small-scale salons, and large-scale conferences.  
Customers: UWA students, staff and alumni; the general Perth public.
Our TEDxUWA conference talks are uploaded to YouTube and TED.com, so our users increase to a more global audience from countries around the world.
Products: TEDxUWA hosts TED-like talks following the ethos of 'ideas worth spreading'. We have three to four small-scale events called TEDxUWASalons, which revolve around a central theme (such as STEM, art, and business). These small events usually have around 70-100 people attending, with 3-4 speakers that give a TEDxUWA talk on a specified theme. We also host full-day conferences that incorporate multidisciplinary talks from a wide range of individuals (including UWA students, staff and alumni, and local Western Australians). The conference aims to spark conversation between many different ideas, people, and practices. 
Competitors: There are currently 8 other TEDx's within WA, that although are not considered our competitors, they work independently of TEDxUWA. For example, there is TEDxKingsPark, TEDxPerth, and TEDxMandurah.
Business Model: Our revenue sources are limited to ticket sales, grants and in-kind support (such as discounts and vouchers, free taster products, and subsidies to event venues) from various organisations.
Challenges: Obtaining consistent and long-term funding to ensure the longevity of our organisation. In addition, securing loyal partners that will assist TEDxUWA in various aspects of events (eg. audiovisual help and merchandise sourcing)

client_background:
Vision: Griefline's vision is to lead and inspire the national mental
health industry in finding collaborative ways
to prevent the escalation of mental health
disorders resulting from grief and loss.
Griefline's mission is to be present for people in a safe space to help
them find courage through their grief and
discover hope and healing after loss. 
Customers: Australians aged between 18 - 85+ who are bereaved, lonely and isolated in their grief seek out Griefline support services.
Victoria and NSW account for largest portion of service users (around 65%)
Stakeholders: Australian Department of Health, South Eastern Melbourne Primary Health Network (SEMPHN), Alfred Health, Community Enterprise Foundation (CEF), Rotary District 9800 (Rotary), Murrumbeena Community Bank (MCB)

Products: National toll-free Helpline, operating 6am - midnight 
Online moderated discussion forums
Grief and loss self help and education resources
Support groups (Vic and NSW only)
Care-to-Call outbound call pilot program (Vic, NSW and FNQ)
Early intervention sessional counselling (VIC and NSW)
Sessional counselling/bereavement support (NSW and VIC) 
Competitors: ACGB (Australian Centre for Grief and Bereavement)
Business Model: Griefline's operations are staffed predominantly by volunteers (Helpline, Care to Call and early intervention grief support programs). 
Revenue sources include grants, donations and sale of grief and loss training programs.
Sessional counselling and bereavement support is staffed by employees (funded by South Eastern Melbourne PHN and NSW Health). 
Admin, Operations, Marketing and Exec team funded by Australian Dept of Health.
Grants and donations fund other programs and online resources

Challenges: Sustainable and ongoing sources of funding. Currently reliant on grants to specific programs, no recurring income streams that meet all organisational costs.

project_scope:
We are looking for the Student Consulting Team to help identify valuable findings and insights for sustainable impact opportunities that are also aligned to our mission.

focus_area:
Reconceiving needs, products and customers to better serve societal needs
In particular in the above, to deliver the most value, we would like the team to focus most effort on;
Identify how Griefline can best measure and demonstrate the value of our existing programs and services
And avoid
Development of new programs and services (unless they also come with option to fully fund)
other_notes:
In any given year in Australia there are approximately 160,000 deaths, 1 in every 5 women who experience a miscarriage, 1 stillborn child per 135 successful births, 1 in every 20 children who experience the death of a parent, 147,000 marriages that result in divorce, and 130,000 people that are diagnosed with cancer (Australian Bureau of Statistics). These statistics are just a snapshot of the difficulties some Australians may endure within their lifetime. There are other unfortunate situations a person could experience resulting in grief and loss.

Grief is a normal human experience. However, if not supported in their grief experience, a percentage of the population will develop prolonged grief disorder. This has been estimated to rise as a result of COVID 
https://www.news.com.au/lifestyle/health/mental-health/demand-for-grief-counselling-over-pandemic-deaths-surges/news-story/84255e2aa8f7ccbaed379a7029196b9d 

Grief-related losses cost U.S. companies as much as $75 billion annually. No research has been conducted to date on the loss to the Australian economy resulting from grief.

client_background:
Vision: Our vision is for a powerful, connected and trusted Pilbara community sector. Pilbara for Purpose is the connector of Pilbara community services, enabling it to achieve best practice, foster effective collaboration and have a voice at the regional level. The P4P Strategic Plan for 2020-2025 articulates the four strategic pillars for our organisation, namely:
Support opportunities for community organisations to work together
Foster best practice locally
Improve Aboriginal engagement in community services
Build an organisational culture of leadership and excellence
Collaboration, connectivity, communication and engagement are common threads throughout the Strategic Plan.

Customers: Pilbara for Purpose is the peak membership body of the for purpose, community services sector in the Pilbara. Our members are not-for-profit, for purpose organisations that deliver a wide range of community services across the Pilbara. Membership is open to organisations that exist primarily for charitable purposes with operations in the Pilbara. Membership is also open to volunteers and individuals who are passionate about excellence in our region and sector.  We also work closely with other stakeholders including industry, local and state government. We have a seat on the Pilbara District Leadership Group.
Products: We provide support to our members in the following ways.

Connect and Collaborate
A key focus of P4P is connecting our Members in person and online.  Events in centres across the Pilbara give an opportunity to meet your peers, build relationships and connect.  P4P Forums are a valuable platform for facilitating networking and encouraging dialogue with decision makers, stakeholders and policy writers; promoting collaboration and communication and focusing on improved outcomes, with a particular focus on place-based solutions and lived experience initiatives. P4P has a Board member based in each of the towns across the Pilbara providing a localised, town based connection for incoming/visiting organisations and individuals.
 	
Have a voice in Pilbara Social Policy Change
Our region is beset with significant change.  P4P has an important seat on the District Leadership Group, providing an opportunity for P4P to be a voice for our Members and the people they serve. P4P will also advocate on behalf of our Members on priority issues that affect our Members and their communities.
 	
Market Yourself and Your Organisation
P4P will offer a range of outlets to celebrate the achievements of our members, spotlight the vibrancy of P4P people, and promote your services and events through our facebook page, website, events and the Pilbara Community Services Excellence Awards.
 	
Be Informed
P4P is a central reference point for practical business and policy information to keep Members informed of policy changes, grant opportunities, career moves, training, outstanding achievement and other topics that keep us connected and informed.
 	
Share
Through P4P you can share ideas, information, and other (non-confidential) resources with peer organisations and like-minded people.  Through our Forums and social media, P4P can support members to share their knowledge, skill base and best practice.
 	
Build Capacity
Through access to information, resources and local sector specific training opportunities, P4P can support you to develop leadership capacity and governance excellence.

Celebrate Excellence
P4P is committed to promoting and celebrating excellence and success within our sector. The Pilbara Community Services Excellence Awards are an ideal opportunity to celebrate Member achievements.
Competitors: We do not really have any competitors. The Western Australian Council of Social Services (WACOSS) has a Pilbara office. WACOSS is the main peak body for the community services sector in the State. However, we work together with WACOSS and try not to duplicate any support we are providing. Many of our members are also members of other peak bodies relevant to their service delivery and many are also members of the local Chamber of Commerce and Industry. We need to be able to demonstrate the value of being a member of our organisation as well as/ or instead of these other organisations.
Business Model: We are reliant primarily on membership fees. We charge an admin fee on any grants/sponsorships which also provides some income. We have been unable to secure any core funding from the government.
Challenges: Financial sustainability. Being reliant on membership fees and at the same time aware that we need to keep our fees low and competitive, we struggle to find the finances needed to deliver the activities/support we would like to deliver for our members. We need to find other ways to fund our activities, either on a project base or in terms of some core funding. We have a dedicated experienced board who are all volunteers and then one part time staff member whose hours depend on the funds available.

client_background:
Vision: Our Vision - is ‘Lives Unaffected by CF’, where every person born with CF will be able to reach their full potential, unencumbered by the disease. Every day we strive a little closer to achieving that vision. 
Our Mission - To provide services to people affected by CF and other respiratory conditions as well as support research, partnering with members, organisations and other stakeholders. 
Customers: People living with Cystic Fibrosis in WA and the support groups. 
Products: Managing the daily treatment of cystic fibrosis (CF) can be quite challenging, whether you are the parent of a newborn, young child, adolescent, or are an adult living with CF. We have a range of support programs to help support you or the person in your life living with CF. Support is available via phone, video call or in the comfort of your own home. 
Home Care Worker Service 
CFWA have a Home Care Worker (HCW) service available in the metropolitan area to provide regular home visits to children and adults with CF. Our HCW program is funded by the Health Department of WA and offers support for airway clearance, nebulisation, exercise, light home duties and occasional respite. Our HCWs receive regular training to provide them with the skills and knowledge they need to supervise treatments safely and effectively. 
Calm Kids, Happy Families Program 
CFWA offer one-on-one support and guidance with our experienced Occupational Therapist (OT), as part of our Calm Kids, Happy Families program. 
Our OT can provide practical solutions and support to the difficulties that can arise in the family system, particularly when also dealing with CF. 
OT's can help you and your child with: 
Developing routines, Calming and de-escalation techniques, Meal and bedtime assistance, Behaviour management techniques, Consistent parenting and Positive communication. 

Building Resilience Program 
Our Youth Social Worker, Haylee, can support young people (~8-11 yrs) through our Building Resilience Program. The one-on-one program uses age-appropriate activities and resources to promote positive mental health, build resilience, address worries and misinformation about CF and encourage independence over CF treatments. 
Getting children used to talking about their feelings at a younger age has positive outcomes for their future mental health. It encourages children to recognise if they are feeling worried and provide them with healthy tools and strategies to process these emotions. 
Hospital Support - Child Support 
Hospital Care Packs and Visits 
We provide Hospital Care Packs to Perth Children’s Hospital (PCH) which are distributed by your CF team as needed. You can ask on the ward if you would like one. 

Financial Support 
We also offer financial assistance for hospital and clinic 
appointments to assist with travel and parking. 
First Hospital Admission 
If your child is admitted to hospital for the first time, we have a ‘My First Admission’ video series which can help you prepare for the admission.

Adult Support - Hospital Food Bag and Visits 
If you’re an inpatient at Sir Charles Gairdner Hospital (SCGH), you can apply online for an Inpatient Food Bag made up of snacks and some toiletry items. 
Financial Support 
We also offer financial assistance for hospital and clinic appointments to assist with travel and parking. 
CFWA Subsidies 
Hospital Parking Subsidy 
CFWA Adult Support Subsidy (16+ years) 
CFWA Family Support Subsidies 
CFWA Equipment 
External Travel Subsidies 
Regional Outreach Program 
Nearly a third of people with cystic fibrosis (CF) in Western Australia (WA) live regionally. Our aim is to bring equitable services and support across the whole of WA. 
Our services team comprise of a social worker/counsellor, 
physiotherapist, community nurse and education officer, which means that we can offer diverse services when we come to your community. 
Regional Subsidies 
Telethon Regional Travel Subsidy 
Adult Regional Travel Subsidy 
Sibling and Offspring Camp Subsidy 
Equipment 
Regional Health Professional Training 
Competitors: None 
Business Model: We have several income sources 
Government contract - 57% 
Donations and Bequests 23% 
Fundraising Events 18% 
Investments 2% 

Challenges: Funding has always been and continues to be our biggest challenge impacting our organisation

client_background:
The Mito Foundation is the only national charity dedicated to supporting people affected by mitochondrial disease (mito) in Australia. It provides support services for patients and their families while increasing awareness and understanding of this devastating disease. The foundation advocates for patient and family needs and funds essential research into the prevention, diagnosis, treatment and cures of mitochondrial disorders.

Mitochondrial disease (mito) is a debilitating genetic disorder that robs the body’s cells of energy, causing multiple organ dysfunction or failure and potentially death. It is highly complex and takes many forms.

One Australian child born each week – that’s 50 each year - will develop a severe or life-threatening form of mito. Mitochondrial disease is terminal; there are no cures and few effective treatments.

Despite being the second most commonly diagnosed, serious genetic disease after cystic fibrosis, public awareness of mito is low compared to other chronic/rare diseases. Many Australians would not have heard of it, and of those that have heard of it, many do not understand what it is.

Whilst Mito Foundation is focused primarily on Australia, it is involved in a number of international collaborations and initiatives with a view to improving outcomes for the global mito community. Global Mitochondrial Disease Awareness Week is one of these global activities.
problem_statement: The client seeks to improve their current market position by exploring innovative solutions to emerging challenges in the industry.
project_scope:
Project title:  Improving the effectiveness of Global Mitochondrial Disease Awareness Week

Background: Each year, the Mito Foundation participates in Global Mitochondrial Disease Awareness Week (GMDAW) – a global initiative held mid-September to raise awareness for this disease.

The key awareness-building activity is Light Up For Mito, whereby monuments around the world are illuminated green. In 2019, 167 monuments participated yet as there was no clear correlation between the monuments illumination in green and the mito cause, its effectiveness as an awareness raising activity was questionable. 

Social Media has been the pivotal channel for the campaign and has been used to:
share facts and statistics of mito (and encourage people to share among their networks)
encourage and share personal stories of patients/families around the world living with mito
promote events and fundraising activities in all the locations of all of the partner organisations, and to
announce new monuments participating in Light Up for Mito as they come on board.

This year, we hope to maximise the awareness raising impact of Light Up for Mito and other facets of the Awareness Week campaign through a coordinated social media campaign that benefits the global mito community and all participating organisations. 

Visit here for more info on the campaign: https://gmdaw.org/light-up-for-mito/

Project objective:

Deliver a social media campaign that delivers on the Global Mitochondrial Disease Awareness Week objectives of raising awareness of mito at both local and global levels whilst building brand awareness of each partner organisation in their local market (as the 

Key challenges:

each partner organization needs to build brand profile and awareness, yet the campaign is hosted on a distinct GMDAW website and social media channels taking people away from the organisations’ existing channels, and introducing a new brand
engaging new followers of the GMDAW website and social media channels as followers of their local brand organization post the campaign
Light Up for Mito is currently the key awareness raising activity of the campaign, but as each partner organization - and mito itself - has relatively low awareness, the public does not see the meaning of a monument turning green

In scope:
New ideas to enhance/adapt the Light Up for Mito activity to improve its effectiveness as an awareness raising tool
Adding/changing past components of the social media strategy to make the campaign more single-minded and effective

Out of scope:
Removing Light Up for Mito from the campaign – this must remain

The Mito Foundation would like the Student Consulting Team to develop a social media strategy that will:
Deliver measurable awareness-building outcomes of Light Up for Mito and the overall Global Mitochondrial Disease Awareness Week campaign
Provide recommendations on content strategy and key messages
Utilise the resources of all participating organisations (see IMP website for members: https://www.mitopatients.org/about/members)
Explore the possibility of adding a fundraising ask to the campaign
This research should be informed by best practices of similar organisations and supplemented by secondary sources.
deliverables:
An oral presentation of the completed project.
A report summarising your findings and recommended strategy for 2020.

Vision: 50% of adolescents and 63% of kids have a fear of needles. 80% of children admitted to hospitals with a chronic illness will experience anxiety from the hospital alone. We have developed an intelligent gamified platform that takes the fear out of nasty medical procedures for children. Comfort Quest delivers personalised learning experiences that leave children feeling confident and in control. For hospital health services, the platform monitors staff performance and patient satisfaction in order to decrease costs and patient flow. 
Customers: Consumers of the application are children and their parents. Customers are healthcare providers, typically hospital organisations that see high volumes of patients each month. We have also partnered with The Meg Foundation to develop best-practices content for the platform.

The plan is delivered to the family and practitioner to work together and provide the best possible medical experience. Children learn simple techniques to manage their anxiety, building lifelong skills that empower them with confidence.

The platform collects data from application usage as well as qualitative feedback from parents to generate valuable analytics and insights for the healthcare provider. client_background:
Source Wellness is one of Australia’s first Juice based functional beverage drinks, where products are designed to better consumers at a cellular level. After extensive research and trials, we developed a unique combination of various unexplored herbs like Ashwagandha, Ginseng, Curcumin, Saffron, Turmeric, Lemon Balm, Skullcap, Liquorice, etc with different juices like Blueberry, Pomegranate, Strawberry to make Functional Beverages. Up till now 3 drink shots of 60ml have been developed each for Energy, Relax, and Gut (+Immune Boost). We are introducing it in Australia.
Issues & Planned Response
People are suffering from various lifestyle diseases which are stopping them from enjoying life, like stress, lack of focus, digestive issues to name a few, available options in the markets like Energy Drinks are filled with chemical ingredients and synthetic caffeine and have various side effects like caffeine disturbs sleep and causes anxiety. Our all-natural drinks help to solve the issues in an all-natural way without any side effects. According to the Australian Bureau of Statistics, almost half (45%) Australians will experience a mental illness in their lifetime (Australian Bureau of Statistics, 2009).
These drinks are the first of their kind in the Australian market. They are made with unexplored all-natural, herbal, nutrient-rich ingredients. The mindful combination of these ingredients with Juices makes them unique and effective, they may help in increased focus, less stress, better digestion, improving immunity, and overall wellbeing.
Targeted Customer Segment
Male and female from age 35+ years. According to Mintel (market research firm), a new generation of consumers are seeking functional and natural alternatives in the UK 40% looking to boost energy levels, 75% Consumers avoiding artificial ingredients, 45% moving away from sports drinks to water, and juices. Therefore, the business will target these customers.

Product Description:
Source Energy: Packed with carefully picked ingredients like Ashwagandha, Ginseng, and Saffron with Pomegranate and Strawberry juices to start the day. Source Energy is a nootropic that aims to enhance cognitive performance, vitality, mental and physical energy and naturally reduce stress to stay focused and energetic throughout the day.

Source Relax contains a blend of delicious ingredients like Lemon Balm, Chamomile, Passion Flower, Skullcap, and Hops with Blueberry juice to support a calm and peaceful state of body and mind and feel energized by unwinding daily.

Source Gut: Rich in prebiotics that improves gut health, Source Gut is diligently developed with Amla, Turmeric, Curcumin, Peppermint, and Liquorice with Mango juice for complete digestive support. Its regular consumption provided relief from gastrointestinal distress apart from increasing energy, improving mood, and boosting immunity.

Due to Covid-19, our whole project got delayed by one year. But the products are designed in such a way that they help to build up immunity.

Please also find the links of our new and old website. We currently have Zoom Drink in market, selling in retail stores, and now we have re-branded the whole range as "Source Wellness"
www.sourcewellness.com.au
www.zoomdrink.com.au

client_background:
Vision: To create a more enjoyable travel experience with a simple, yet effective lock for every backpacker.
Mission: To add security and peace of mind in every travel with a simple, affordable, and light-weight lock for every backpacker.

Buckle Lock is a travel accessory for backpackers, designed specifically to secure side release buckles found on many top-loading backpacks. It can also be used to secure buckles on other types of bags, such as camera bags, and cycle bags. 
The business is currently in pre-revenue stage. The business started just before the pandemic and was put on hold during the pandemic. Currently, we are wanting to know if the market is ready for a travel related product; and if so, recommendations to launch the business during a pandemic.
Buckle Lock is a lock to secure a backpack. Backpacks come in two kinds – with zips, or with side release buckles. Backpack with zips are easy to secure as you can use any padlock in the market. That is not the case for side release buckles that are found on many top-loading backpacks. The unique design of a buckle means that you cannot use a padlock to secure it. This leaves a lot of backpackers vulnerable to theft. Buckle Lock (patent pending) is designed to secure the side release buckles on a top-loading backpack, thereby stopping a thief from entering the backpack and stealing valuable belongings.
Our main competition is Pacsafe. They sell a lot of travel security related products. One product in particular, backpack wire mesh, covers the entire backpack. The other competitor is Osprey Airporter (Deuter also have a similar product), which is essentially a duffel bag inside which the backpack is stored, and the zips of the duffel bag are locked.
There used to be a similar product to our’s in the market before, Pacsafe BuckleSafe. This product is discontinued.

client_background:
Conexie is an energy optimising software. Rather than waiting for a bill and trying to rectify the problem after already realising the significant cost, we give our customers the power to act on energy data in real-time so that they can use their own energy to maximise its value.

By making your energy consumption and production insights readily available, Conexie provides the right information to the right people in real-time through our native smart phone apps. The completely customisable backend has a suite of communication tools that allow for automated triggers to alert and connect the right groups or individuals when there is abnormal activity. Once this trigger has occurred, our solution allows for collaboration in a dedicated environment. This tool is a critical factor in the pursuit of optimising the OPEX or ROI of energy related assets.

We currently facilitate the energy communication for two ASX listed companies and have channel partners that use us as part of their sales process for solar installations. This means we are on-boarding around 10-15 new sites a week, both in residential and commercial.

On the back of this success, we are now seeing a lot of inbound opportunities in other verticals where our communication solution is just as applicable, this is something we are excited to explore moving forward alongside our core business.

client_background:
The Communication Research Institute (CRI)—a not for profit membership organization—started 35 years ago and has accumulated an international reputation for its work in communication and information design: making print and digital documents usable and acceptable to people. CRI has worked at the interface between large organisations and the publics they serve. 

The types of documents have included forms, bills, letters, notices, contracts, product descriptions, instructions, procedures, and many more. 
CRI has worked for government and business, across a spectrum of agencies and industries. Its work is evidence-based and has resulted in significant ROI for its clients. At the heart of our work is an evidence-based set of methods which eclipse such things as design thinking, codesigning, participative design and other fashionable design processes and methods.
CRI’s overall purpose is to bring the standard of communication between organisations and the publics they serve to an acceptable level.

For an overview see: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405872618300194

CRI’s main income has in the past come from projects commissioned by large organisations. For many reasons, this income source has declined, and the standard of public documents have also declined, with most organisations not achieving an acceptable level of performance in creating information that people can use, with the consequent disadvantages to people and measurable productivity losses to organisations.
Given our mission, we are turning to other activities that will sustain an income for CRI and allow us to grow the body of supporters who will be able to use our methods and advocate for government and business reform.
The main thrust of this change in our activity is to become a publishing house, making our methods, case histories, and research findings attractive, available, and usable by students, consultants and workers in large organisations.
